explanation: Microwaves have short wavelength, hence energy dispersion during long distance communication is very low. Thus, they are used in radar system.
name: Questions Item 1

text: Radiations of intensity 0.5 Wm⁻² are striking a metal plate, the pressure on the plate is
explanation:
Intensity of radiation,

\( I = 0.5 \) Wm⁻²

Pressure on the plate due to radiation is given by

\( p = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{0.5}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.166 \times 10^{-8} \) Nm⁻².
name: Questions Item 2

text: Practically ozone layer absorbs radiations of wavelength
explanation: Practically ozone layer absorbs ultraviolet radiations coming from sun and wavelength of ultraviolet radiation become less than 3 × 10⁻⁷ m.
name: Questions Item 3

options: an uncharged stable particle at rest
explanation: When a charge moves with acceleration, then it produces a time varying electric and magnetic field. Hence, electromagnetic waves are produced by accelerated or deaccelerated charges only.
name: Questions Item 4

text: If a source is transmitting electromagnetic wave of frequency 8.2 × 10⁶ Hz, then wavelength of the electromagnetic waves transmitted from the source will be
explanation:
Frequency of electromagnetic wave,

\( \nu = 8.2 \times 10^6 \) Hz

Wavelength, \( \lambda = \frac{c}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{8.2 \times 10^6} = 36.5 \) m.
name: Questions Item 5

text: Pick out the longest wavelength from the following types of radiations.
explanation:
Ultraviolet light is electromagnetic radiation with a wavelength shorter than that of visible light, but longer than X-rays.

\( \therefore \lambda_{\text{red}} > \lambda_{\text{blue}} > \lambda_{\text{UV}} > \lambda_{\text{X-ray}} \)

Hence, visible red light has longest wavelength.
name: Questions Item 6

text: Light is an electromagnetic wave. Its speed in vacuum is given by the expression
explanation:
Speed of light (EM wave) in vacuum or air is given as

\( c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} \)

where, \( \mu_0 \) = permeability of free space and \( \varepsilon_0 \) = permittivity of free space.
name: Questions Item 7

text: For television broadcasting, the frequency employed is normally in range
explanation: Generally, 30 to 300 MHz frequency is used for the television broadcasting signals. This range of frequency is called VHF (Very High Frequency) band.
name: Questions Item 8

text: An electromagnetic wave of frequency \( 1 \times 10^{14} \) Hz is propagating along z-axis. The amplitude of electric field is 4 Vm⁻¹, then energy density of the electric field will be
explanation:
Given, electromagnetic wave frequency, \( f = 1.0 \times 10^{14} \) Hz

amplitude of the electric field, \( E_0 = 4 \) Vm⁻¹

The value of energy density (energy/volume) is given by

\( u = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E_0^2 = 70.4 \times 10^{-12} \) Jm⁻³.
name: Questions Item 9

text: A lamp delivers a luminous flux of 100 W to an absorber of area 1 cm². The force due to radiation pressure is
explanation:
Force = Rate of change of linear momentum

\( F = \frac{1}{c} \frac{dU}{dt} = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{100}{3 \times 10^8} = 3.3 \times 10^{-7} \) N.
name: Questions Item 10

text: Which of the following is/are the property/properties of a monochromatic electromagnetic wave propagating in the free space?
options: 1. Electric and magnetic fields will have a phase difference \( \frac{\pi}{2} \).
2. The energy of the wave is distributed equally between electric and magnetic fields.
3. The pressure exerted by the wave is the product of its speed and energy density.
4. The speed of the wave is equal to the ratio of the magnetic field to the electric field.
explanation: The energy of electromagnetic wave is equally distributed between electric and magnetic field.
name: Questions Item 11

text: The amplitude of the electric field in a parallel beam of plane electromagnetic waves of intensity 53.1 W m⁻¹ is (Permittivity of free space = \( 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \) C²N⁻¹m⁻²)
explanation:
Intensity of electromagnetic wave,

\( I = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E_0^2 c \)

\( E_0 = 200 \) NC⁻¹.
name: Questions Item 12

explanation:
A → III (Gauss's law for electricity)

B → I (Gauss's law for magnetism)

C → II (Faraday's law)

D → IV (Ampere-Maxwell law).
name: Questions Item 13
